content:
# Acts Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 232-234 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_007_page_001.jpg, ACT_007_page_002.jpg, ACT_007_page_003.jpg

---

**[1]** The high priest said, "Is this true?"

**[2]** Stephen answered: "Brothers and fathers, hear me out. The God of glory appeared to our ancestor Abraham while he was still in Mesopotamia, before he ever settled in Haran,

**[3]** and told him, 'Leave your country and your relatives, and go to the land I will show you.'

**[4]** So he left the land of the Chaldeans and settled in Haran. After his father died, God relocated him to this very land where you are living now.

**[5]** Yet God gave him no inheritance in it — not even a foot of ground to call his own. But he promised to give it to him as a possession, and to his descendants after him, even though at the time Abraham had no child.

**[6]** God told it to him straight: 'Your descendants will be strangers in a foreign land, and the people there will enslave them and brutalize them for four hundred years.

**[7]** But I will judge the nation that enslaves them,' God said, 'and after that they will come out and worship me in this place.'

**[8]** Then God gave him the covenant of circumcision. And so when Abraham became the father of Isaac, he circumcised him on the eighth day. Isaac became the father of Jacob, and Jacob became the father of the twelve patriarchs.

**[9]** "The patriarchs, consumed with jealousy, sold Joseph into Egypt. But God was with him

**[10]** and rescued him from all his troubles. God gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who appointed him ruler over Egypt and over his entire household.

**[11]** Then a famine struck all of Egypt and Canaan, bringing terrible suffering, and our ancestors could not find food.

**[12]** When Jacob heard there was grain in Egypt, he sent our ancestors on a first trip.

**[13]** On the second visit, Joseph revealed his identity to his brothers, and Joseph's family became known to Pharaoh.

**[14]** Joseph then sent word and invited his father Jacob and his whole family — seventy-five people in all — to come to Egypt.

**[15]** So Jacob went down to Egypt. He died there, as did our ancestors.

**[16]** Their bodies were brought back to Shechem and placed in the tomb that Abraham had purchased for a sum of silver from the sons of Hamor in Shechem.

**[17]** "As the time drew near for God to fulfill his promise to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt,

**[18]** until a different king rose to power in Egypt — one who knew nothing about Joseph.

**[19]** This king exploited our people and oppressed our ancestors, forcing them to abandon their newborn infants so they would not survive.

**[20]** It was at this time that Moses was born — and he was no ordinary child. For three months he was nurtured in his father's house.

**[21]** When he was set outside, Pharaoh's daughter took him in and raised him as her own son.

**[22]** Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and became powerful in both his words and his actions.

**[23]** "When he turned forty, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the people of Israel.

**[24]** Seeing one of them being mistreated, he went to his defense and avenged the oppressed man by striking down the Egyptian.

**[25]** He assumed his brothers would understand that God was using him to deliver them, but they did not understand.

**[26]** The next day he came upon two of them fighting and tried to reconcile them, saying, 'Men, you are brothers! Why are you hurting each other?'

**[27]** But the one who was wronging his neighbor shoved Moses aside and said, 'Who made you a ruler and judge over us?

**[28]** Are you going to kill me like you killed the Egyptian yesterday?'

**[29]** At this, Moses fled and became an exile in the land of Midian, where he fathered two sons.

**[30]** "After forty years had passed, an angel appeared to him in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, in the flame of a burning bush.

**[31]** Moses was astonished at the sight. As he approached to get a closer look, the voice of the Lord came to him:

**[32]** 'I am the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' Moses trembled with fear and did not dare to look.

**[33]** The Lord said to him, 'Take the sandals off your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy ground.

**[34]** I have seen — I have truly seen — the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their groaning, and I have come down to set them free. Now come, I am sending you to Egypt.'

**[35]** "This Moses whom they had rejected, saying, 'Who made you a ruler and judge?' — this is the very one God sent as both ruler and redeemer, through the angel who appeared to him in the bush.

**[36]** He led them out, performing wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness for forty years.

**[37]** This is the Moses who told the people of Israel, 'God will raise up a prophet for you from among your own brothers, just as he raised up me.'

**[38]** This is the one who was in the assembly in the wilderness with the angel who spoke to him at Mount Sinai, and with our ancestors. He received living words to pass on to us.

**[39]** "But our ancestors refused to obey him. Instead, they pushed him away and in their hearts turned back to Egypt,

**[40]** telling Aaron, 'Make us gods who will lead us. As for this Moses who brought us out of Egypt — we have no idea what has happened to him!'

**[41]** That was when they fashioned a calf-idol, offered sacrifice to it, and threw a celebration for what their own hands had made.

**[42]** So God turned away from them and handed them over to worship the host of heaven — just as it is written in the book of the prophets: 'Did you bring me sacrifices and offerings during those forty years in the wilderness, house of Israel?

**[43]** No — you carried along the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Rephan, the images you made to worship. So I will exile you beyond Babylon.'

**[44]** "Our ancestors had the tent of testimony in the wilderness, just as God directed when he told Moses to make it according to the pattern he had seen.

**[45]** Our ancestors who received it brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations that God drove out before them. It remained until the time of David,

**[46]** who found favor before God and asked to find a dwelling place for the God of Jacob.

**[47]** But it was Solomon who actually built a house for him.

**[48]** "Yet the Most High does not live in houses made by human hands. As the prophet says:

**[49]** 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool. What kind of house will you build for me, says the Lord, or what place could serve as my resting place?

**[50]** Did not my hand make all these things?'

**[51]** "You stubborn people! Your hearts and ears are still uncircumcised! You are always fighting against the Holy Spirit — just like your ancestors did, so do you!

**[52]** Which of the prophets did your ancestors not persecute? They killed those who predicted the coming of the Righteous One — and now you have become his betrayers and murderers,

**[53]** you who received the law delivered through angels and yet did not keep it!"

**[54]** When they heard this, they were cut to the heart with fury, and they ground their teeth at him.

**[55]** But Stephen, filled with the Holy Spirit, gazed intently into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God.

**[56]** He said, "Look! I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!"

**[57]** At that, they covered their ears, let out a great shout, and rushed at him all together.

**[58]** They dragged him outside the city and began to stone him. The witnesses laid their cloaks at the feet of a young man named Saul.

**[59]** As the stones rained down, Stephen called out, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit!"

**[60]** Then he fell to his knees and cried out with a loud voice, "Lord, do not hold this sin against them!" And when he had said this, he fell asleep.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 2 — "hear me out"** (ακουσατε): The Greek imperative "listen!" is rendered conversationally as "hear me out" to capture the urgency and gravity of Stephen's opening — he knows this is a defense speech before a hostile court.

2. **Verse 4 — "relocated him"** (μετωκισεν): The Greek verb carries the sense of a deliberate, God-directed resettlement, not merely a casual move. "Relocated" captures the divine agency behind the migration.

3. **Verse 6 — "told it to him straight"**: Dynamic rendering of God's direct speech introduction. The Greek simply uses ελαλησεν ("spoke"), but the content of what follows is a blunt, sobering prophecy — the tone warrants directness.

4. **Verse 9 — "consumed with jealousy"** (ζηλωσαντες): More than mere jealousy — the patriarchs' envy was the driving force behind selling their brother. The intensified rendering captures the emotional weight the Greek participle carries as the cause of the betrayal.

5. **Verse 19 — "forcing them to abandon their newborn infants"** (εκθετα ποιειν τα βρεφη): The Greek εκθετα literally means "exposed" — the practice of leaving infants outside to die. The dynamic rendering makes the horrific reality explicit for modern readers.

6. **Verse 20 — "no ordinary child"** (αστειος τω θεω): Literally "beautiful to God" or "divinely beautiful." The Sinaiticus reading preserves this striking phrase — Moses was extraordinary in God's eyes from birth. "No ordinary child" conveys the same sense naturally.

7. **Verse 34 — "I have seen — I have truly seen"** (ιδων ειδον): The Hebrew-influenced doubling construction (infinitive absolute) emphasizes intensity: God has not merely noticed — he has seen with purpose and will act. The repetition is preserved for rhetorical force.

8. **Verse 35 — "ruler and redeemer"** (αρχοντα και λυτρωτην): λυτρωτης means one who ransoms or liberates. "Redeemer" captures both the liberation and the theological weight Stephen is building toward — connecting Moses typologically to Christ.

9. **Verse 38 — "living words"** (λογια ζωντα): Stephen calls the Sinai revelation "living oracles" — not dead letters but words pulsing with divine life. This is a deliberate rhetorical move: the very law his accusers claim he blasphemes, he honors as alive.

10. **Verse 41 — "threw a celebration"** (ευφραινοντο): Rendered dynamically to convey the festive, self-indulgent nature of the idol worship — they were not merely "rejoicing" but reveling in their own handiwork.

11. **Verse 51 — "stubborn people"** (σκληροτραχηλοι): Literally "stiff-necked" — an image of an ox that refuses the yoke. "Stubborn" communicates the defiance; "uncircumcised hearts and ears" is preserved as Stephen deliberately weaponizes covenant language against covenant people.

12. **Verse 57 — "covered their ears"**: The Sinaiticus text vividly portrays the council's visceral reaction — they physically block out Stephen's words before attacking. This detail underscores the irony of verse 51: their ears are indeed uncircumcised.

13. **Verse 60 — "fell asleep"** (εκοιμηθη): The earliest Christian euphemism for death. Stephen does not merely "die" — he sleeps, with all the resurrection hope that implies. This gentle verb stands in shocking contrast to the violence of the stoning, and is preserved exactly.

14. **Structural note:** Stephen's speech is the longest in Acts and follows a deliberate rhetorical arc — Abraham (promise), Joseph (rejection and vindication), Moses (rejection, deliverance, and prophetic foreshadowing), the tabernacle versus the Temple (true worship), and finally the explosive accusation (verses 51-53). Each section builds toward the climax: Israel's pattern of rejecting God's chosen agents culminates in their betrayal of the Righteous One. The dynamic translation preserves this momentum through pacing and sentence structure.

— *PNEUMA*
